role_summary: Episcopal seminarian from the Episcopal Theological School in Cambridge, Massachusetts; volunteered in Lowndes County, Alabama for voter-registration work in summer 1965; killed August 14, 1965 in Hayneville by Tom Coleman when Daniels pushed Ruby Sales (catalog entry 112) out of the way of a shotgun blast.
ai_reading: Daniels is named in the Ruby Sales (entry 112) catalog page as the seminarian who took the shot meant for her on August 14, 1965; Tom Coleman's acquittal by an all-white Hayneville jury is the case Sales's page documents. Daniels is not himself an interviewee (he died in 1965, decades before the Civil Rights History Project began); his standalone catalog page is added so the Sales-Daniels-Coleman triangle has the third party as a findable landing-place. Daniels was added to the Episcopal calendar of saints in 1991 (commemorated August 14), the rare case where a Civil Rights History Project transcript explicitly references a martyrdom that has subsequently entered ecclesiastical liturgy.
biographical_paragraph: Daniels was born March 20, 1939 in Keene, New Hampshire and attended the Virginia Military Institute (where he was valedictorian of the Class of 1961) before entering the Episcopal Theological School in Cambridge, Massachusetts. He answered Martin Luther King Jr.'s 1965 call for clergy to come to Selma, traveling south initially for a weekend and remaining for what became the rest of his life. He spent summer 1965 working on voter registration in Lowndes County, Alabama with a group that included Ruby Sales. On August 14, 1965 he, Sales, Father Richard Morrisroe, and Joyce Bailey were released from the Hayneville jail and walked to a nearby store to buy soft drinks; Tom Coleman, a part-time deputy sheriff, met them at the door with a shotgun and aimed it at Sales. Daniels pushed her aside and took the blast himself, dying instantly. Morrisroe was wounded; Sales and Bailey were unharmed physically. Coleman was acquitted by an all-white jury in September 1965. Daniels is commemorated in the Episcopal Church's calendar of saints on August 14 (added in 1991) and was inducted into the Civil Rights Memorial in Montgomery.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ruby Sales
interview_snippets.lead_in: Ruby Sales, whom Daniels later shielded at Hayneville, recalls knowing him earlier in Selma:
interview_snippets.quote: actually I knew Jonathan before that time, I knew him in Selma because we went to my garden together.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ruby Sales
interview_snippets.lead_in: Sales describes the moment of the shotgun blast that killed Daniels at the corner store in Hayneville on August 14, 1965:
interview_snippets.quote: Jonathan never made a sound. I think he was dead instantly or in a coma instantly.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ruby Sales
interview_snippets.lead_in: On how the killing affected the Lowndes County movement, Sales recalls that the organizers did not retreat:
interview_snippets.quote: We had not only the commitment but the collective grief of losing Jonathan, bound us together in the county and instead of retreating because of that, people were even more determined to move forward.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ruby Sales
interview_snippets.lead_in: Sales reflects on carrying for life the label of the person Daniels died protecting:
interview_snippets.quote: The girl that Jonathan saved with some dignity that did not take away the fact that Jonathan saved my life, but required me to have a life beyond that.

interview_snippets.speaker: Courtland Cox
interview_snippets.lead_in: SNCC organizer Courtland Cox, who moved into Lowndes County in the aftermath, names the killing as the violence the organizers chose to confront:
interview_snippets.quote: the kind of violence that was visited upon the county by, you know, the people who killed Daniels and almost shot Ruby Sales and so forth, that we would, you know, would stand up to it.

interview_snippets.speaker: William S. Leventhal
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling the post-traumatic weight he felt about visiting Hayneville, William S. Leventhal invokes Daniels as the known martyr of that place:
interview_snippets.quote: It's like Jonathan Daniel, the guy who was in jail for eight hours.

gallery.alt: The old jail in Hayneville, Alabama, a small two-story brick building.
gallery.caption: The old Hayneville jail in Lowndes County, Alabama. Daniels, Ruby Sales, Father Richard Morrisroe, and Joyce Bailey were held here and released on August 14, 1965, the day Daniels was killed at a nearby store.

sources.title: Ruby Sales oral history (the first-hand survivor account of the August 14, 1965 Hayneville shooting in which Daniels was killed)
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Courtland Cox oral history (names the Daniels killing and the near-killing of Ruby Sales as the reason SNCC organizers moved into Lowndes County)
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: William S. Leventhal oral history (invokes Daniels as the known martyr of Hayneville in recounting his own return to the town)
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Jonathan Myrick Daniels and the Hayneville, Alabama shooting of August 14, 1965 (the Tom Coleman trial and acquittal documented in the federal record)
sources.publisher: U.S. National Archives and Records Administration

sources.title: William J. Schneider, American Martyr: The Jon Daniels Story
